question: Explain the core principle behind the Bubble Sort algorithm. Describe its best-case and worst-case time complexity, and briefly explain what scenarios lead to each.
expectedPoints: Core principle: Repeatedly steps through the list, compares adjacent elements and swaps them if they are in the wrong order. This pass is repeated until the list is sorted.
Best-case time complexity: O(n), which occurs when the list is already sorted and the algorithm makes a single pass with no swaps.
Worst-case time complexity: O(n²), which occurs when the list is sorted in reverse order, requiring maximum possible comparisons and swaps.

question: Contrast the Selection Sort and Bubble Sort algorithms. Identify and explain one key advantage Selection Sort has over Bubble Sort in terms of its operational efficiency.
expectedPoints: Fundamental Difference: Selection Sort works by repeatedly finding the minimum element from the unsorted part and putting it at the beginning. Bubble Sort works by repeatedly swapping adjacent elements if they are in the wrong order.
Key Advantage: Selection Sort makes a strictly limited number of swaps (O(n) swaps) regardless of the initial data, as it only swaps once per element to place it in its final position.
Operational Impact: This makes Selection Sort more efficient than Bubble Sort (which can require O(n²) swaps) in scenarios where the cost of swapping items is high (e.g., large records).

question: State the main disadvantage shared by both Selection Sort and Bubble Sort, making them inefficient for large datasets.
expectedPoints: Their main disadvantage is their quadratic time complexity, O(n²), for average and worst-case scenarios.
This means that as the input size (n) doubles, the number of operations required roughly quadruples, leading to very slow performance on large lists.
This inefficiency is due to their nature as comparison-based sorting algorithms that do not utilize a divide-and-conquer strategy or other optimizations.
